All right, welcome back to the show. In this lesson, we're gonna talk about what's essentially going on in the moments before you get on an enrollment call, right? And we're also gonna look at some of the common fears that may be spinning through your mind that may be a little bit suppressed that are actually creeping up and making those calls a little less fun, relaxed, and ultimately fulfilling and successful as maybe they could be. So this is all about those pre-call mindsets and fear clearing, and we're gonna begin with a little bit of a self-check, right? Put yourself in this situation right now. So it's 9.58 AM. You have a sales call on your 5K program at 10 AM. So in two minutes, you're gonna be dialing somebody up or opening up that Zoom room, whatever that looks like. How do you feel? How do you feel good? Do you feel expansive? Do you feel light? Do you feel peaceful? Do you feel excited? Or do you feel a little bit contracted? A little bit nervous? Do you need to pee? Are you rushing to the bathroom? Right now, to get one last pee in before 10 AM strikes. Just be honest with yourself. How do you feel, right? And that honest starting point is really going to be the gateway to essentially creating new pre-call routines, mindsets, and clearing some of those fears that may actually be leading to that I need to pee feeling. So yeah, that's what we're gonna be going. That's what's going down in this lesson. Let's begin. This is really gonna be about the 9010 that will have you showing up with that confidence, with that lightness, and just brimming with positive power about what's possible for your client and what's possible for your ability to serve them. So five core pre-call mindsets we're gonna be discussing the first one, a transparent starting point, detaching from the result, changing the frame, connecting to your confidence, and being in the right energy. So let's begin. Transparent starting point. This is our starting point here, right? If you can make sure that your pre-client is undoubtedly aware that the intention of this call is to explore their eligibility, qualification, or fit into your program, and see if you can help them, everything else downstream is gonna feel that much easier. So to make this practical, avoid the bait and switch or framing. This has a coaching call or a strategy call or anything. That can be ambiguous and create a sense of being blindsided. Exploring eligibility into a program should be framed as the privilege that it actually is, right? So much of this nervousness and anxiety comes from this notion and knowledge that your prospect, the other person on that call, may not even be aware that there's gonna be a program pitched at the end of it, and you know that there's gonna be this inevitable, awkward moment where you all of a sudden have to transition it to that moment, and you're gonna feel their discomfort with it. And that's kind of what you want to avoid and defend against and what you don't want to inflict on them, right? So having this transparent starting point is gonna make you feel that much better about getting on the call. Your confidence will come from knowing that they want to be there, that they have a desire around what you do, and they're excited about the potential of being extended an invitation if it's a fit. Now this is the same concept that we run in our webinars, right, making it abundantly clear that there's going to be an offer here, there's gonna be an invitation. Putting that wherever you can, right, on the booking call page, in the emails, and the communications leading up to it, and then even on the call itself at the beginning, and then permission before you get to the offer to begin with, right? So having these ample opportunities for your prospect to know exactly what they're getting into, and you knowing that they want to be there, that they want to receive it, that they want to qualify for that invitation and have it extended if things make sense, that's going to work best for everybody. Cool, so having a clear, transparent pre-call, pre-frame will clear most of the anxiety you feel and give you a very easy low-friction opener, right? The opener becomes a very easy. So tell me, what inspired you to jump on this call to explore automated intimacy? What's been resonating with you? What kind of made you want to jump on and explore fit on this program? It becomes natural, it becomes easy, and it enters the conversation from a place of just mutual understanding of what this conversation is going to be about. So if your current calls aren't abundantly clear what they're about, that's really going to be the first step before going into the other elements of this lesson. But the next one is detaching from the result, right? The best way to be disarming on your calls and create safety isn't to be passive and meek and loose and allowing them to call all the shots, it's actually to just de-arm yourself of your own needs, your fears, your projections, and how that might leak into how you actually show up on the call. So forget about your own stakes, right? Especially your need or your possible need for the sale to happen, right? This might sound easier said than done, there are ways to work around that. But your stakes, what you're coming armed to the call with is what makes people feel defensive. Even if they can't even understand it on a mental level, they'll certainly feel it. So, next, while detaching from that result, acknowledge that there's a version of them that joins, right? If they're on that call and you had a transparent starting point, there is a version of them that joins your program and you can coach the transformation to help them arrive there, but you don't have full control over their coachability or willingness or ability to arrive there at the end of this call. So acknowledge, right, that there is an element of the unknown here. There are parts of this that you don't control. You don't control how quick they are to transform you. You don't control how easy it will be or won't be for them to unlock and move through some of the resistance. All you can do is show up, acknowledging that there is a version of them that is capable of making this decision. You are gonna show up in the service of coaching that, but you can only do so much, right? It is advanced, it is co-creative, and that's why so much of what we're gonna talk about later in this program is about getting them resourceful around co-creating aspects of that relationship. So claim full responsibility for what you can offer and do and acknowledge and be okay with the lack of complete control in the process. Decharge some of your fears around it, right? When we talk about coming to these calls armed, right, and armored, a lot of these are just fears that haven't been acknowledged and expressed in our own bodies, right? So these are some examples of fears that I've discovered are present within myself that I've worked to unlock and unwind and release. I've recommended resource for that once again, a world without fear by John Jones, where he has an incredible technique for actually untangling some of these fears within ourselves. Another more popular version is the Sedona method, which you may or may not be familiar with, but also highly recommended. So some of the fears that you may have, and it's important to keep a checklist, right? If you are feeling that pre-call anxiety, write it down, journal around it, see what's present there, don't suppress it and stuff it and charge yourself up fully and pretend it's not there. You're better off dismantling and disengaging what has been bubbling up under the surface than just trying to suppress it and show up with this false confidence and bravado that everyone could just kind of see through, including yourself. So some typical fears I've encountered within myself is I am my fear that clients name won't like me or feel unsafe with me, right? A lot of us have this need to be liked and for others to feel safe in our presence. I am my fear that I won't be able to get the sale and as a result, my business will X, right? The consequences of not getting the sale. We wanna D-charge and disarm that fear. I am my fear that I won't close the sale and I'll feel like a loser or failure or weak. I'll have to tell my business coach that I didn't get the sale and I'll get shamed and feel ridiculous and small and embarrassed and all these things, right? So how it actually makes us look in our own self perception and how we fear others will perceive us for our failure. I am my fear that if I ask difficult questions, I'll get a negative or aggressive reaction. A lot of us are a little bit non-confrontational. We hate conflict. We wanna avoid at all costs and we'll avoid asking powerful and necessary questions out of fear of someone else reacting poorly to our question. For those who are more empathic, if you will, I am my fear of being overwhelmed by somebody's energy. I am my fear that I don't have what it takes to powerfully lead this conversation. I am my fear that I'll say the wrong thing and X person will think I'm stupid and not qualified to lead them. I am my fear that I don't have the skills to actually lead this person. I am my fear that client will think I'm greedy, selfish, not worth it when I drop my price. I am my fear of even dropping my price. I am my fear and shame around having such a high price. And I am my fear of sharing my price and causing stress for clients' names. So all of these are potential fears that are lingering right under the surface, right? And these leak into how you show up on these calls. They leak into what you are or aren't willing to do in that sacred container and ecosystem. And there's certainly sabotaging your ability to create empowered conversations and empowered decisions for your client. So they are worth disarming, they are worth examining and it's all in service, right? Of feeling incredible on these calls, which you are meant to feel incredible on. Next, change the frame. So acknowledge this isn't a zero sum conquest where a win is a sale and anything else is a loss. Make it simply about an exploration of fit. A successful call is where a decision has been made based on the factuality of whether or not a fit exists or not. Cool, so show up in service of exploring that fit. Loss aversion versus gain attainment. Don't start with the idea that the sale is something that can be lost, but as something that can be gained. So you can't lose what doesn't exist yet. The sale doesn't exist, that fit hasn't been established. You can't lose a sale, you can only gain a sale. And it sounds a bit like mental gymnastics, but there's actually truth in it. You can't lose what doesn't exist. You can only gain what hasn't yet manifested. So come at it with that mindset. The call itself is a process of alchemy where you're working to turn a potentiality into a reality. This is pure magic, right? Have fun in that transformative process and relish in your own power and ability to guide that transmutation, to turn potential into reality is one of the most exhilarating aspects of our creative power that we have, especially in relationship to others where we can do that dance together in an incredible, fun, empowering way. So enjoy it. Next, very practical. Connect to your authentic confidence in your program, right? Review testimonials, review wins, review success stories. Bask in the enjoyment of having others transform their lives or businesses or whatever it is through your help. And know that it works. Know that you are successful at this. Know that you are a good coach, a good leader, a good service provider, whatever that is. Know that you've done this and you've done this well and people have deeply, deeply appreciated what you've been able to do. Connect to your authentic confidence in your pre-customer's abilities, right? Don't project your own lack of confidence on them, right? Everyone is different. They all have their own abilities, readiness to succeed. So review their application, review the conversation you've already had with them and connect to elements of them that give you confidence in their ability to succeed with you and your program. And based on both those, one and two, connect to your authentic confidence of the fit between your prospect and your program. So if you can give each of these a score from one to 10, if it totals less than 20 to 30, simply make the goal of the call an exploration to see if you can bring yourself to a place of confidence, right? It's about exploring fit. It's about ultimately feeling confident enough to extend that invitation. And you don't need to be fully confident. You need to be confident enough and we're gonna talk all about ways to present that invitation with your own reservations. If you're not fully confident, doing it. If you feel like there could be a little bit of a mismatch in fit, that's okay to bring to the table even in the context of an invitation. We're gonna talk about how to practically do that. But before the call, connect your confidence in your program, in yourself, your abilities, your pre-customer's abilities and the fit between all of those things. And make your call about seeing if you could get that confidence level between all three, two somewhere between that 20 to 30 range. Cool. Sales call anxiety typically comes from the fear that they might not be the right fit and that you, because you're armed with your own needs, would try to force it anyway. That's typically where sales call anxiety comes from. You almost want to defend your prospect from you and from a bad decision right out of the gates, which is what creates dissonance from the opening beat where you're split, where you can't be of leadership to them when you're attached to that result and you feel less than confident that they're going to make a good decision based on the way you're going to lead it because you're reading off of the script that once again forces compliance rather than explores fit. So be in the right energy. Remember the call should be starting with a degree of desire and inspiration of them wanting to be there and wanting to be extended in invitation. Take five to 10 minutes before your call to breathe, move, sing, dance, do yoga, do chi gong, meditate, dance, whatever it needs, whatever you need to do to get some energy moving in your body to be more dynamic, to be more expressive in your own body, in your own being, to be more confident in your own body. I do have a Spotify playlist. Maybe I'll share it one of these days. But yeah, Spotify playlist are cool. Cold War Kids, great pre-call music. Anyway, set your intention. I have an epic natural conversation with Alex or whatever their name is and empower him or her to make a clear and confident decision in my program. That's really what this is all about. You don't want to go into these calls feeling like they're going to be contractive. Open up to the possibility that it might be and it should be the best part of your day, the best part of your week, the best conversation you've had all month because it's certainly possible, especially if you do things right to get people on the calls. If you're transparent in what this is, if you're really attracting the right people, these calls are incredible opportunities to go deep and really be of service to people you care about because you've chosen to devote your life and career to serving. Cool, bonus. The observer effect, right? How would you show up? If you assumed that they're already a customer, right? And you're just here to coach them on, the onboarding call, right? And the reason this is powerful is people tend to orient around how you perceive them and treat them. So treat them as if they are someone who has already invested in you, right? And hint, they already have, right? If they've invested this much time, energy, and resource in talking to you, right? And all the things that led to that, they've already invested quite a bit in you, right? And if you acknowledge that, if you talk to someone as if they have, right? They're gonna become that much more likely to live into that relationship. So that's what I got. That's the pre-com mindsets. Biggest key here is be attuned to what is driving anxiety in you, don't be afraid of it, put it on paper, journal on it, and recognize that the more you could de-arm yourself in advance of these calls, the lighter, more expansive, and more empowering they're ultimately gonna be. Cool, so that's all we got for this. Take a breather, and I'll meet you in the next lesson. Bye for now.
